region_of_origin: China (Hunan / Sichuan tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese stir-fried yellow beef. FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Beef meat, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Chinese-style stir-fried yellow beef with aromatics.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried yellow beef is approximately 300 g, intended as a shared main dish alongside rice for 2–3 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables, Chinese style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; adapted with reference to HPB Singapore nutrient data for Chinese stir-fried beef dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Signature Stir-Fried Yellow Beef'. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for stir-fried beef with vegetables (Chinese style, NDB 35193 / similar SR Legacy entries) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for Chinese beef stir-fry dishes. Yellow beef (huáng niú) is a lean, grass-fed cattle breed common in Hunan and Sichuan cooking; its nutritional profile is comparable to lean stir-fried beef. Cooking oil, soy sauce, and aromatics are factored into the composite estimate. Values represent a cooked, plated dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for stir-fried beef with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese stir-fried yellow beef. GI is estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous category for mixed meat-and-vegetable stir-fry dishes with soy sauce and minimal starch. Lean beef has a negligible GI contribution; the small amount of cornstarch and oyster sauce raises the composite GI modestly above pure protein foods. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 12.6) / 100 ≈ 3.8, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.2 × 3 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1560 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a full restaurant serving may contribute approximately 1560 mg sodium — over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) with high protein supporting satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried yellow beef has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar sauces and paired with a low-GI carbohydrate staple.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; lean yellow beef provides beneficial iron and B12
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat and high sodium per restaurant serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose steamed rice over fried accompaniments, and request minimal oil and sauce to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Yellow Beef (小炒黄牛肉, xiǎo chǎo huáng niúròu) is a classic Chinese stir-fry dish originating from the Hunan and Sichuan culinary traditions, widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore. The dish features thinly sliced yellow beef (huáng niú), a lean, grass-fed cattle breed prized for its tender texture and clean flavour, wok-fried at high heat with dried chillies, celery, garlic, ginger, and spring onions, seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, and lightly bound with cornstarch. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying in a very hot wok — produces a dish with a slightly charred, smoky aroma (wok hei), tender beef slices, and a savoury, mildly spicy sauce. The texture is firm yet succulent, with a balance of umami, heat, and fragrance. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate option providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, 16.2 g protein, and 7.8 g fat. It is a good source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin. The sodium content is elevated due to soy and oyster sauces (~520 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is low (~30), making it suitable for blood glucose management when paired with an appropriate carbohydrate staple. In Singapore, this dish is commonly ordered as a shared main course alongside steamed jasmine rice or congee.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Signature Stir-Fried Yellow Beef' exists in HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy stir-fried beef with vegetables (Chinese style) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for comparable Chinese beef stir-fry dishes. Yellow beef (huáng niú) is nutritionally comparable to lean stir-fried beef. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for mixed meat-vegetable stir-fry. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or HPB Singapore source data for this dish category. 2. The dish is listed as 'Signature Stir-Fried Yellow Beef' on field survey data at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Stir-Fried Yellow Beef' per Veritas naming rules. 3. Halal status is not confirmed; the restaurant (Wan Jia Fu) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment on the MUIS Singapore registry as of the processing date; is_halal is set to false. 4. Nutrient values represent the composite cooked dish per 100 g including sauce and aromatics. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 12.6) / 100 ≈ 3.8. 6. The food_id GD003804 is preserved from the input seed record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from seed input. Added all multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Singaporean cuisine compliance. Adapted nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy and HPB Singapore. Set GI to 30 (analogous estimate, University of Sydney GI Database). Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only. Scored 8/8 on first complete draft.

Iteration 2: Verified all hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria. Confirmed aliases consistency check: all strings in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) appear verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed GI category 'low' correct for GI=30 (≤55 threshold). Confirmed -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Final decision: PASS.
